one_line_takeaway: A 2024 survey that maps machine unlearning end-to-end — taxonomizing exact vs. approximate (and centralized vs. federated) algorithms, sharply separating owner-facing verification from provider-facing evaluation metrics, cataloguing LLM/recommender/IoT applications and MU-as-defense, and cataloguing the attacks (membership inference, slow-down, over-unlearning) that the unlearning process itself invites.
key_finding: As a survey the central 'finding' is a landscape map with several critical positions: (1) verification metrics (data-owner-facing) and evaluation metrics (provider-facing) are fundamentally different and must not be conflated — a gap the authors say every prior survey (Table 1) leaves at least partly uncovered. (2) Exact unlearning gives a clean theoretical deletion guarantee but is computationally intractable for deep/non-convex models; approximate unlearning is cheap and scales to complex models but only offers statistical guarantees and may leave sensitive residue in parameters. (3) The common definition of approximate unlearning as 'parameter indistinguishability from a retrained model' is contested — reaching a given point in parameter space is neither sufficient (Thudi et al.: identical models arise from disjoint datasets) nor a reliable benchmark (Tarun et al.). (4) Distributed/federated unlearning is far less mature than centralized; server-side FU is fast but incomplete (residue remains, needs stored historical updates), while local-side FU is more complete but bounded by edge-device compute. (5) Unlearning is double-edged: it protects privacy yet the act of forgetting itself opens new attack surfaces (membership inference, slow-down/DoS, over-unlearning).
core_contribution: This is a survey (not a new method or system) of machine unlearning: the problem of making an already-trained ML model 'forget' a subset of its training data D_u so that the resulting model is indistinguishable from one retrained from scratch on the remaining data D_r, as required by 'right to be forgotten' regulations (GDPR, CCPA, CPPA). The authors' OWN contributions (Section 1.1, Table 1) are: (1) a fine-grained taxonomy of unlearning algorithms split into exact unlearning (conventional convex-model-specific vs. complex non-convex-model-specific) and approximate unlearning (data-driven: data-isolation vs. data-modification; model-driven: influence-function, Fisher/FIM, distillation, gradient-based); (2) a dedicated analysis of distributed/federated unlearning (server-side vs. local-side) and its three unique challenges; (3) a taxonomy that explicitly separates VERIFICATION metrics (used by the data owner to check the provider's claim: invasive/watermark-based vs. non-invasive/membership-inference, data-reconstruction, cryptographic) from EVALUATION metrics (used by the model provider: effectiveness, efficiency, utility) — a distinction the authors argue prior surveys conflate; (4) a survey of MU-enabled applications (LLMs, recommender systems, IoT, and MU as passive/active defense); and (5) a categorization of attacks that specifically target the unlearning mechanism. It also summarizes the emerging 'debate' that indistinguishability-in-parameter-space is neither necessary nor sufficient for genuine unlearning (Thudi et al., Tarun et al.).
threat_model: The survey discusses two distinct adversary settings rather than proposing one. (A) Honest-but-untrustworthy model provider: providers claim to have removed D_u but may not (to conserve resources, retain valuable user data, or avoid accuracy loss); this motivates VERIFICATION metrics that let the data contributor independently check genuine deletion, including stealthy watermarking/backdoor injection before training, membership-inference probes, data-reconstruction, and cryptographic proof (Eisenhofer et al.'s SNARK+hash-chain protocol). (B) Adversaries attacking the unlearning mechanism itself (Section 7, Fig. 7, Table 9): (i) MU-specific membership inference — a black-box adversary compares the original vs. unlearned model outputs (or injects perturbations, Lu et al.'s FP2-MIA) to infer whether a sample was in the forgotten set D_u, exploiting the 'Streisand effect' where over-unlearning leaks the very data it meant to protect; (ii) data-poisoning / slow-down attacks (Marchant et al., white/grey-box) that inflate unlearning cost like a DoS, and camouflaged poisoning (Di et al., grey-box) that makes the unlearned model misclassify targets; (iii) over-unlearning attacks (Hu et al., black-box) where a malicious user requests unlearning of specially blended data to degrade model utility beyond the intended scope.
limitations: By its own account (Section 8) the field it surveys is immature: no unified, deployable verification standard exists (watermark-based checks damage accuracy and embed exploitable backdoors; cryptographic proofs are opaque to non-expert users); most unlearning targets classification, with regression/generative and feature-/task-level unlearning under-explored; distributed unlearning is largely limited to federated learning, leaving split/peer-to-peer/collaborative settings open; and current FU cannot jointly achieve effectiveness and efficiency. As a survey the paper itself presents no empirical validation of the trade-offs it tabulates (advantage/drawback and resource ratings are qualitative), performs no reproduction of the surveyed results, and its comparisons rest on the cited authors' own reported claims. Coverage stops in early 2024.

quantitative_results: Survey scope: covers machine unlearning literature spanning 2015-2024, with ~140 references (bibliography [1]-[141]).

Table 1 compares this survey against 10 prior machine-unlearning surveys ([98] 2021, [75] 2022, [79] 2022, [133] 2023, [83] 2023, [120] 2023, [123] 2023, [67] 2023, [92] 2023, [119] 2024) across 17 coverage dimensions (invasive/non-invasive verification, effectiveness/efficiency/utility evaluation, exact/approximate taxonomy, debate, distributed unlearning, applications FU/LLMs/RES/IoT, defenses PD/AD, and attacks MIA/DPA/OUA); the authors mark their survey ('Ours') as the only one covering all 17 columns.

Taxonomy enumerates 2 top-level algorithm classes (exact, approximate); approximate is split into 2 data-driven strategies (isolation, modification) and 4 model-driven strategies (influence-function, Fisher/FIM, distillation, gradient).

Section 7 / Table 9 catalogues 3 MU-specific attack families across 5 works: membership inference ([20] black-box, [69] FP2-MIA black-box), data poisoning ([72] slow-down white/grey-box, [24] camouflaged grey-box), and over-unlearning ([48] black-box).

No experimental/quantitative measurements are produced by the authors; all numbers in the paper are qualitative classification tables (Tables 1, 3-9) and formal notation (Eqs. 1-3, e.g. the Anamnesis Index AIN and influence/Fisher update rules), not benchmark results.
